title: Procedural Monster Generation with Stat-Based Additive XP Scaling

requirements.text: Expand the monster roster in frontend/src/data/monsters.ts to include a diverse set of prefix and suffix pools (e.g., prefixes: 'Ancient', 'Infernal', 'Cursed', 'Withered', 'Verdant', 'Dread', 'Spectral', 'Molten', 'Forsaken', 'Elder'; suffixes: 'Goblin', 'Warlord', 'Shade', 'Colossus', 'Serpent', 'Revenant', 'Wraith', 'Titan', 'Fiend', 'Elder God'). Each prefix and suffix must carry a stat modifier object (multipliers or flat bonuses for HP, attack, and defense) so that the generated monster's final stats are a combination of its base template stats and both the prefix and suffix modifiers. The `generateMonsterName` (or equivalent) utility must produce a unique monster name and resolved final stats each time it is called.

quotes: create a sample set using suffixes and prefixes to make a unique monster every time
Procedural monsters built from prefix + suffix combinations (e.g., "Ancient", "Infernal", "Cursed" + "Goblin", "Warlord", "Elder God") — each with stats influenced by their modifiers

acceptanceCriteria: At least 10 prefixes and 10 suffixes are defined, each with a stat modifier object containing HP, attack, and defense values.
Calling the monster generation function twice produces monsters with different names and/or stats in the vast majority of cases.
A generated monster's HP, attack, and defense stats visibly reflect the combined modifiers of its prefix and suffix (e.g., 'Infernal' prefix boosts attack, 'Colossus' suffix boosts HP and defense).
Monster names displayed in combat follow the 'Prefix Suffix' pattern (e.g., 'Ancient Elder God').

requirements.text: Update the combat XP reward calculation in frontend/src/engine/combatEngine.ts to use an additive formula: Final XP = base XP (derived from monster level) + flat bonus per HP point + flat bonus per attack point + flat bonus per defense point. All three stat bonuses must contribute equally (same per-point bonus rate). This means a monster with high HP, high attack, AND high defense awards significantly more XP than a low-stat monster of the same level.

quotes: XP rewards will scale based on a combination of monster level AND monster stats (defense, HP, attack power, etc.)
Additive formula (base XP + bonuses per stat)
base XP (from monster level) + equal flat bonuses for HP, attack, and defense stats

acceptanceCriteria: The XP formula is clearly additive: Final XP = baseXP(level) + (hpBonus * monsterHP) + (atkBonus * monsterAttack) + (defBonus * monsterDefense), where hpBonus, atkBonus, and defBonus are equal constants.
A high-defense 'Ancient Elder God' awards significantly more XP than a low-stat 'Weak Goblin' at the same monster level.
XP reward shown in the combat log and DungeonRunScreen reflects the new formula.
The bonus constants are tuned so stat contributions are meaningful but not so extreme that they overshadow level-based scaling.

requirements.text: Update DungeonRunScreen and DungeonSelectScreen to use the new procedural monster generation system. When a dungeon run begins, generate a monster using the prefix+suffix system at the selected monster level, then run combat against that procedurally generated monster (with its combined stats). Display the generated monster's full name (e.g., 'Infernal Titan') in the combat log and any monster stat previews.

quotes: Procedural monster generation using prefix + suffix combinations
tougher stat combos = more XP, so a high-defense Elder God pays out far more than a weak Goblin at the same level

acceptanceCriteria: Each dungeon run generates a new monster with a procedurally combined name and stats.
The monster's full 'Prefix Suffix' name is shown in the combat log.
The combat engine receives the generated monster's final stats (HP, attack, defense) and uses them for the fight.
XP awarded at the end of the run reflects the generated monster's actual stats via the new additive formula.

constraints: All stat bonus rates for HP, attack, and defense in the XP formula must be equal (same per-point value).
Do not modify any files listed in frontend.immutablePaths.
Backend data model should not need changes; all monster generation and XP calculation logic stays on the frontend.
nonGoals: Persistent monster definitions stored in the backend.
Unequal stat weighting in the XP formula.
Changes to loot drop rates or loot generation logic.
